Built with doc-gen4, running Lean4. Bubbles () indicate interactive fragments: hover for details, tap to reveal contents. Use Ctrl+↑Ctrl+↓to navigate, Ctrl+🖱️to focus. On Mac, use Cmdinstead of Ctrl.

import Mathlib.Init.Logic

section needs_better_home
/- This section contains items that have no direct counterpart from Lean 3 / Mathlib 3.
   They should probably probably live elsewhere and maybe in some cases should be removed.
-/

/-- An identity function with its main argument implicit. This will be printed as `hidden` even
if it is applied to a large term, so it can be used for elision,
as done in the `elide` and `unelide` tactics. -/
@[reducible] def 
hidden: {α : Sort u_1} → {a : α} → α
hidden {
α: Sort u_1
α : 
Sort _: Type u_1
Sort
Sort _: Type u_1
 _} {
a: α
a : 
α: Sort u_1
α} := 
a: α
a

C: Sort u_1

e: Empty
e => match 
e: Empty
e with.

Empty: Type
Empty := ⟨λ
a: Empty
a => 
a: Empty
a.

p →

p :=

Decidable.by_contradiction

-- alias by_contradiction ← by_contra
theorem 
by_contra: ∀ {p : Prop}, (¬p → False) → p
by_contra {
p: Prop
p} : (¬
p: Prop
p → 
False: Prop
False) → 
p: Prop
p := 
Decidable.by_contradiction: ∀ {p : Prop} [φ : Decidable p], (¬p → False) → p
Decidable.by_contradiction

library_note "decidable namespace" /--
In most of mathlib, we use the law of excluded middle (LEM) and the axiom of choice (AC) freely.
The `decidable` namespace contains versions of lemmas from the root namespace that explicitly
attempt to avoid the axiom of choice, usually by adding decidability assumptions on the inputs.

You can check if a lemma uses the axiom of choice by using `#print axioms foo` and seeing if
`classical.choice` appears in the list.
-/

library_note "decidable arguments" /--
As mathlib is primarily classical,
if the type signature of a `def` or `lemma` does not require any `decidable` instances to state,
it is preferable not to introduce any `decidable` instances that are needed in the proof
as arguments, but rather to use the `classical` tactic as needed.

In the other direction, when `decidable` instances do appear in the type signature,
it is better to use explicitly introduced ones rather than allowing Lean to automatically infer
classical ones, as these may cause instance mismatch errors later.
-/

-- See Note [decidable namespace]
protected theorem 
Decidable.not_not: ∀ [inst : Decidable a], ¬¬a ↔ a
Decidable.not_not [
Decidable: Prop → Type
Decidable 
a: Prop
a] : ¬¬
a: Prop
a ↔ 
a: Prop
a :=

Iff.intro: ∀ {a b : Prop}, (a → b) → (b → a) → (a ↔ b)
Iff.intro 
Decidable.by_contradiction: ∀ {p : Prop} [φ : Decidable p], (¬p → False) → p
Decidable.by_contradiction 
not_not_intro: ∀ {p : Prop}, p → ¬¬p
not_not_intro

/-- The Double Negation Theorem: `¬ ¬ P` is equivalent to `P`.
The left-to-right direction, double negation elimination (DNE),
is classically true but not constructively. -/
@[simp] theorem 
not_not: ∀ {a : Prop}, ¬¬a ↔ a
not_not : ¬¬
a: Prop
a ↔ 
a: Prop
a := 
Decidable.not_not: ∀ {a : Prop} [inst : Decidable a], ¬¬a ↔ a
Decidable.not_not

theorem 
of_not_not: ¬¬a → a
of_not_not : ¬¬
a: Prop
a → 
a: Prop
a := 
by_contra: ∀ {p : Prop}, (¬p → False) → p
by_contra

/-- Transfer decidability of `a` to decidability of `b`, if the propositions are equivalent.
**Important**: this function should be used instead of `rw` on `decidable b`, because the
kernel will get stuck reducing the usage of `propext` otherwise,
and `dec_trivial` will not work. -/
@[inline] def 
decidable_of_iff: {b : Prop} → (a : Prop) → (a ↔ b) → [inst : Decidable a] → Decidable b
decidable_of_iff (
a: Prop
a : 
Prop: Type
Prop) (
h: a ↔ b
h : 
a: Prop
a ↔ 
b: Prop
b) [
Decidable: Prop → Type
Decidable 
a: Prop
a] : 
Decidable: Prop → Type
Decidable 
b: Prop
b :=

decidable_of_decidable_of_iff: {p q : Prop} → [inst : Decidable p] → (p ↔ q) → Decidable q
decidable_of_decidable_of_iff 
h: a ↔ b
h

/-- Transfer decidability of `b` to decidability of `a`, if the propositions are equivalent.
This is the same as `decidable_of_iff` but the iff is flipped. -/
@[inline] def 
decidable_of_iff': (b : Prop) → (a ↔ b) → [inst : Decidable b] → Decidable a
decidable_of_iff' (
b: Prop
b : 
Prop: Type
Prop) (
h: a ↔ b
h : 
a: Prop
a ↔ 
b: Prop
b) [
Decidable: Prop → Type
Decidable 
b: Prop
b] : 
Decidable: Prop → Type
Decidable 
a: Prop
a :=

decidable_of_decidable_of_iff: {p q : Prop} → [inst : Decidable p] → (p ↔ q) → Decidable q
decidable_of_decidable_of_iff 
h: a ↔ b
h.
symm: ∀ {a b : Prop}, (a ↔ b) → (b ↔ a)
symm

/-- Prove that `a` is decidable by constructing a boolean `b` and a proof that `b ↔ a`.
(This is sometimes taken as an alternate definition of decidability.) -/
def 
decidable_of_bool: (b : Bool) → (b = true ↔ a) → Decidable a
decidable_of_bool : ∀ (
b: Bool
b : 
Bool: Type
Bool), (
b: Bool
b ↔ 
a: Prop
a) → 
Decidable: Prop → Type
Decidable 
a: Prop
a

true: Bool

h => 
isTrue: {p : Prop} → p → Decidable p

h: true = true ↔ a
h.

1

rfl)
| 
false: Bool

mt

2

/-- One of de Morgan's laws: the negation of a conjunction is logically equivalent to the
disjunction of the negations. -/
theorem 
not_and_distrib: ∀ {a b : Prop}, ¬(a ∧ b) ↔ ¬a ∨ ¬b

/-! ### Declarations about equality -/

section equality

variable {
α: Sort ?u.10739
α : 
Sort _: Type u_1
Sort
Sort _: Type u_1

a: α
a 
b: α
b : 
α: Sort ?u.10601

theorem 
heq_iff_eq: ∀ {α : Sort u_1} {a b : α}, HEq a b ↔ a = b
heq_iff_eq : 
HEq: {α : Sort u_1} → α → {β : Sort u_1} → β → Prop
HEq 
a: α
a 
b: α
b ↔ 
a: α
a = 
b: α
b :=
⟨
eq_of_heq: ∀ {α : Sort u_1} {a a' : α}, HEq a a' → a = a'

heq_of_eq: ∀ {α : Sort u_1} {a a' : α}, a = a' → HEq a a'

theorem 
proof_irrel_heq: ∀ {p q : Prop} (hp : p) (hq : q), HEq hp hq

/--
Extract an element from a existential statement, using `Classical.choose`.
-/
-- This enables projection notation.
@[reducible] noncomputable def 
Exists.choose: {α : Sort u_1} → {p : α → Prop} → (∃ a, p a) → α
Exists.choose {
p: α → Prop
p :

α → 
Prop: Type

P: ∃ a, p a

a: α
a, 
p: α → Prop
p 
a: α
a) : 
α: Sort u_1
α := 
Classical.choose: {α : Sort u_1} → {p : α → Prop} → (∃ x, p x) → α
Classical.choose 
P: ∃ a, p a
P

/--
Show that an element extracted from `P : ∃ a, p a` using `P.some` satisfies `p`.
-/
lemma 
Exists.choose_spec: ∀ {α : Sort u_1} {p : α → Prop} (P : ∃ a, p a), p (choose P)

choose: {α : Sort u_1} → {p : α → Prop} → (∃ a, p a) → α

section ite

/-- A function applied to a `dite` is a `dite` of that function applied to each of the branches. -/
lemma 
apply_dite: ∀ {α : Sort u_1} {β : Sort u_2} (f : α → β) (P : Prop) [inst : Decidable P] (x : P → α) (y : ¬P → α),
  f (dite P x y) = if h : P then f (x h) else f (y h)

f (dite P x y) = if h : P then f (x h) else f (y h)

/-- A function applied to a `int` is a `ite` of that function applied to each of the branches. -/
lemma 
apply_ite: ∀ {α : Sort u_1} {β : Sort u_2} (f : α → β) (P : Prop) [inst : Decidable P] (x y : α),
  f (if P then x else y) = if P then f x else f y
apply_ite {

α

β :

f :

P] (

y: α
y :

ite

y) = 
ite: {α : Sort u_2} → (c : Prop) → [h : Decidable c] → α → α → α

apply_dite: ∀ {α : Sort u_1} {β : Sort u_2} (f : α → β) (P : Prop) [inst : Decidable P] (x : P → α) (y : ¬P → α),
  f (dite P x y) = if h : P then f (x h) else f (y h)
apply_dite

/-- Negation of the condition `P : Prop` in a `dite` is the same as swapping the branches. -/
@[simp] lemma 
dite_not: ∀ {α : Sort u_1} (P : Prop) [inst : Decidable P] (x : ¬P → α) (y : ¬¬P → α),
  dite (¬P) x y = dite P (fun h => y (_ : ¬¬P)) x

/-- Negation of the condition `P : Prop` in a `ite` is the same as swapping the branches. -/
@[simp] lemma 
ite_not: ∀ {α : Sort u_1} (P : Prop) [inst : Decidable P] (x y : α), (if ¬P then x else y) = if P then y else x
ite_not {

  dite (¬P) x y = dite P (fun h => y (_ : ¬¬P)) x
